q: How long does the Japan student visa process take?
a: The Japan student visa process typically takes 2–4 months from application to approval. This includes the Certificate of Eligibility (COE) from Japan (6–8 weeks) and the visa stamp at the embassy in Kathmandu (1–2 weeks).

q: Do I need to know Japanese before applying?
a: Most Japanese language schools accept beginners. However, for direct university admission, JLPT N2 or N3 is usually required. We offer Japanese language classes from N5 level to help you prepare.

q: Can I work while studying in Japan?
a: Yes. International students in Japan are allowed to work up to 28 hours per week during the academic term and full-time during holidays. Many students cover a significant part of their living expenses through part-time work.

q: What is the MEXT scholarship and how do I apply?
a: MEXT is the Japanese Government Scholarship offered to outstanding students. It covers tuition, living allowance, and airfare. Applications are typically in April–May. We provide full guidance for MEXT applicants.

q: What documents are required for a Japan student visa?
a: Key documents include: completed visa application form, passport, photos, COE from Japan, school acceptance letter, financial proof (bank statements), academic certificates, and a medical certificate. Our team prepares all of these for you.

q: How much does it cost to study in Japan from Nepal?
a: Total first-year costs typically range from NPR 1,600,000 to NPR 2,900,000, which includes consultancy fees, language courses, tuition, accommodation, living expenses, and airfare. Japan is significantly more affordable than the USA, UK, or Australia.

q: What are the intakes for Japan?
a: Japan has two main intakes: April (spring) and October (autumn). The April intake is larger and has more institution options. Applications typically open 6–8 months in advance.

q: Does Abroad Sewa offer post-arrival support?
a: Yes! Our support continues after you land. We have an alumni network in Japan who help new students with city hall registration, bank accounts, SIM cards, and settling in. You are never alone.
